the proper celebration of Labor
Day have been completed and the
turnout of the local unions in the
parade promises to be the largest
in the history of the city. Through
some little misunderstanding when
the committees were first appoint
ed 1t was thought that there would
be a falling off in the number of
unions that void participate In the
celebration, these little difficulties
have been settled in satisfactory
manner and complete understand
ing arrived at between all the local
unions.


Among the speakers who will ad-
dress the picnickers at Mantle
park is the noter Chicago attorney,
Clarence Darrow, who has been
retained to defend the imprisoned
officers of the Western Federation
of Miners. Mr.. Darrow was the
former law partner of Governor
Altgeld and has been retained
many times by labor organizations
in its fights in the courts. He is
said to be one of the best posted
men on the labor question in the
country.


AS outlined in the official pro-
gram the parade will march from
the corner Of Monroe and Front
at 10 o'clock Monday in two divis-
ions. The first will form on First
avenue west of Monroe and the
second on First east of Monroe.
The unions will march in the fol-
lowing order:


First Division.
Marshal of the day, w. F. Simons.
Squad of police.


Band.


Speakers in carriages.
Aide, George G. Cody.
Typographical union.
Talors.


Cigarmakers.
